entry.title: PV_DC_driver
entry.summary: PV_DC_driver Controller for a PV installation heating a hot water storage tank. Project in development and initial testing phase. Authors: Hardware - Paweł Z. Software - Arkadiusz K. Heater control (on/off) --> MPPT PWM 12V DC power supply - external adapter Maximum PV panel voltage:…
entry.content:
PV_DC_driver

Controller for a PV installation heating a hot water storage tank. Project in development and initial testing phase.

  1. Features
  2. Power Supply
  3. Enclosure
  4. MPPT Heater Control
  5. Voltage Measurement
  6. Current Measurement
  7. Temperature Measurement
  8. Control
  9. Pinout

Authors: Hardware - Paweł Z.    Software - Arkadiusz K.

Features

  • Heater control (on/off) --> MPPT PWM
  • 12V DC power supply - external adapter
  • Maximum PV panel voltage: 250V
  • Voltage measurement
    • on the PV panel
    • on the heater
  • Current measurement
    • on the heater
  • Power measurement
    • calculated from heater voltage and current
  • Temperature measurement
    • water in the hot water storage tank
    • in the controller enclosure / heatsink
  • Control
    • from the controller level -> display and buttons
    • over LAN - website or application
  • Data logging and visualization on the server
  • Hermetic enclosure

Power Supply

The controller will be powered from an external 12V DC voltage source with minimum 1A. Protection in the form of a medium-slow blow fuse 800mA 5x20mm.

  • Power Supply
  • Fuse

Enclosure

  • Hermetic box ZP180.120.90JPH TM ABS-PC KRADEX
    • Dimensions
  • Aluminum heatsink
    • Thermal resistance: 2 K/W
    • Dimensions: 150 x 50 x 25 mm
  • Round monostable button
    • 250V / 2A
    • LED backlight 2V
    • Mounting hole: 16mm
    • Mounting depth 20mm
    • Monostable
  • Microphone connector GX12 plug + socket
    • Mounting hole: 12mm
  • PG16 cable gland
    • Type: PG16
  • DC power socket 2.1/5.5mm
    • Inner pin diameter: 2.1mm
    • Outer pin diameter: 5.5mm
    • Mounting hole: 8mm
  • Backlit switch
    • 250V / 1.5A
    • LED backlight 2V
    • Mounting hole: 16mm
    • Mounting depth 25mm
    • Bistable
    • Documentation and dimensions
  • LCD display 16x2 with i2c converter
    • Controller: HD44780
    • i2c converter: PCF8574
    • Documentation and dimensions
  • 12V Buzzer
    • Power supply 9-15V
    • Diameter: 12mm
    • Height: 7.5mm

MPPT Heater Control

A PV panel behaves like a current source within a certain range. With excessive loading, voltage decreases and consequently power also decreases. With insufficient loading, voltage increases but current decreases, so power also decreases. Therefore, the load must be adjusted so that the maximum power of the panels is utilized at any given moment. We cannot regulate the heater resistance which is 'fixed', so we must regulate the current. Professional inverters use switching power supplies for this purpose, which allow voltage regulation on the load (thus also current regulation). Unfortunately, a power converter of about 2kW is too complex and the benefits of using it in our project are disproportionate to the time and effort. We will use PWM control.

Schematic diagram of heater control

  • PWM duty cycle: 70%
  • Panel current: 7A
  • Heater resistance: 17.63Ohm

The transistor switch will operate at 10kHz frequency, so by adjusting the duty cycle of the square wave we will be able to regulate the average current flowing[...] To prevent voltage changes on the PV panels from following PWM voltage changes, a capacitor bank must be used as an energy buffer. When the heater is off, energy from the panels will be stored in the capacitors (panels will charge the capacitors to their voltage). When the heater turns on, the capacitors will supply additional current and their voltage will not drop dramatically. In this way, we can regulate the average current of the load and the average voltage of the PV panels.

Through voltage and current measurements, we will learn the power at which the heater operates. To find the optimal operating point of the circuit, we will use an algorithm written in Python.

  • Transistor switch
    • Two parallel-connected N-mosfet transistors WMJ25N80M3
    • Galvanic isolation using optocoupler
    • Control voltage 12V
    • N-mosfet driver built from bipolar transistors

N-mosfet transistor driver

To ensure the fastest possible switching of the transistor switch, we will use a current buffer/amplifier, which will provide sufficiently high gate charging current. An additional stage with [...]

Voltage Measurement

ADC converter characteristic in ESP32

The ADC converter in ESP32 is not linear. Through experiments, it was found that 130mV should be added to the measurement result (operates in the range 130mV - 2500mV)
ADC converter calibration must be performed!!! Calibration instructions using the 'ESP32_ADC_calibration_tool'

Voltage measurement circuit on PV panels

  • Voltage measurement on the panel (buffer capacitor) using voltage divider (1:98.6)
    • Relatively precise voltage measurement in the range 13V - 250V (we assume maximum voltage 250V, while below 40V voltage we disable water heating)
    • Resistor values must be large so that large currents do not flow
    • R1=976kOhm, R2=10kOhm
    • For input voltage Uwe=250V, output voltage is Uwy=2.535V (according to simulation)
  • RAIL-TO-RAIL amplifier acting as voltage buffer
  • Low-pass RC filter with fg=33.86Hz (R=10kOhm, C=470nF)

Real-world tests

Tests will be performed on the finished prototype controller with attached panels.
At this point, it is known that the RC filter (R=10kOhm, C=470nF) behaves correctly at f=10kHz

Current Measurement

UNDER TESTING

Current measurement circuit - ACS712

  • For current measurement, we use the ACS712 sensor
    • Shop link
  • Low-pass RC filter with fg=33.86Hz (R=10kOhm, C=470nF)
  • *ACS712 can measure current flowing in both directions. For 0A current, it sets 2.5V on the output. Since ESP32 operates with 3.3V logic, we will connect the current converter 'in reverse' (0-20A -> [...]

UNDER TESTING

Current measurement circuit - 10mOhm shunt

  • To measure the current flowing through the heater, we will use a 10mOhm resistor as a series shunt.
  • Low-pass RC filter with fg=33.86Hz (R=10kOhm, C=470nF)
  • Using a non-inverting amplifier we will amplify the signal 10 times and shift (and invert) the signal by 0.5V up (2.5V - 0.5V --> 0A - 20A), so we will work in lin[...]
  • A protective stage (R14, D3, D4) was added against high voltage at the input of the first operational amplifier (in case the measurement resistor burns out, at the input of the op-amp there[...]

Real-world tests

  • Current measurement using the ACS712 circuit works much better at 3kHz PWM frequency (instead of 10kHz)

  • RC filter (R=10kOhm, C=470nF) and ADC calibration work at f=3kHz

  • ACS712 tested on a breadboard is very sensitive to external interference (including touching it)

  • Measurement accuracy obtained (voltage) approx. 100mV (approx. 1A)

  • Building a prototype measurement circuit on a 10mOhm shunt is in progress. We will check how the circuit behaves and with what accuracy it measures the flowing current.

Temperature Measurement

  • Water temperature in the boiler measured using DS18B20 sensor in a hermetic enclosure
    • To reduce the risk of incorrect reading (if the sensor is damaged), two such sensors will be used
    • Mounting using GX12 connectors
  • Temperature measurement inside the enclosure/heatsink also using DS18B20

Control

ESP32 Pinout

entry.title: Android Remote RC Control via UDP
entry.summary: Android Remote RC Control via UDP A lightweight Android application that enables remote control of an RC vehicle using UDP communication protocol. The app provides an intuitive interface with dual analog joysticks and toggle buttons for basic vehicle movement and auxiliary controls. This is a…
entry.content:
Android Remote RC Control via UDP

A lightweight Android application that enables remote control of an RC vehicle using UDP communication protocol. The app provides an intuitive interface with dual analog joysticks and toggle buttons for basic vehicle movement and auxiliary controls.

Project Overview

This is a mobile control application built with B4A (Basic4Android) framework that communicates with an RC vehicle receiver (or any UDP-enabled device) via network sockets. The vehicle acts as a UDP server receiving control commands, while the app sends movement and state data in JSON format.

Key Features

  • Dual Joystick Control: Two independent analog joysticks for intuitive vehicle maneuvering

    • Left joystick: Vehicle power/thrust control
    • Right joystick: Steering/turning control

  • Auxiliary Buttons: Three toggle buttons for additional functions

    • Button 1, 2, 3: Custom vehicle functions (lights, special modes, etc.)

  • Battery Monitoring: Real-time battery level feedback from the remote vehicle

    • Receives battery status via UDP in JSON format
    • Displays current battery percentage

  • Network Configuration UI:

    • Manual IP address configuration
    • Custom remote port selection
    • Local listening port setup
    • Input validation (IPv4 format, port ranges 1-65535)

  • Landscape Mode: Full-screen optimized interface for comfortable landscape gameplay

How It Works

Communication Protocol

  1. Initialization Phase:

    • User enters remote device IP address and port in the main configuration screen
    • App validates IP format and port ranges
    • Proceeds to the control screen upon successful validation

  2. Control Transmission:

    • Joystick and button inputs are captured in real-time
    • Data is packaged into JSON format with the following structure: 
      {
  "Pos_X": -100 to 100,      // Steering input
  "Pos_Y": -100 to 100,      // Power/speed input
  "Btn1_state": true/false,  // Button 1 toggle state
  "Btn2_state": true/false,  // Button 2 toggle state
  "Btn3_state": true/false   // Button 3 toggle state
}
       
    • JSON payload is sent via UDP to the configured remote address and port

  3. Status Reception:

    • App listens for UDP packets on configured local port
    • Receives battery status from remote device in JSON format: 
      {
  "Battery_level": 0-100
}
       
    • Displays battery percentage in real-time on the control screen

Technical Details

  • Framework: B4A (Basic4Android) - Cross-platform rapid development framework
  • Protocol: UDP (connectionless, low-latency)
  • Data Format: JSON (human-readable, easily extensible)
  • Orientation: Landscape only (optimized for vehicle control)

Project Structure

B4A/               # Android application source (B4A project)
B4XMainPage.bas    # Configuration screen with IP/port setup
B4XPilotPage.bas   # Control screen with joysticks and buttons
Android_Remote_UDP.b4a  # Main project file
 

Usage

  1. Configure Connection:

    • Enter the IP address of your RC vehicle/receiver
    • Specify the remote port the vehicle is listening on (default: 1900)
    • Set a local port to receive battery feedback (can be 0 for auto-assignment)
    • Tap "Connect" to proceed

  2. Control Vehicle:

    • Use left joystick to control forward/backward movement
    • Use right joystick to control steering left/right
    • Toggle Btn1, Btn2, Btn3 for auxiliary functions
    • Watch battery level in real-time

  3. Return to Configuration:

    • Tap the back button to return to the connection screen
    • Modify settings and reconnect as needed

Input Validation

The app includes robust input validation:

  • IP Address: Standard IPv4 format validation using regex
  • Remote Port: Range 1-65535
  • Listen Port: Range 0-65535 (0 = auto-assign)

Dependencies

  • B4A core library
  • Network library (UDP support)
  • JSON library (data serialization)
  • B4XPages library (UI page management)
  • Joystick wrapper library (analog control)
  • Collections library (data structures)

Potential Applications

  • RC car remote control
  • Drone/quadcopter control
  • Remote robot operation
  • IoT device manipulation
  • Network-based device control experiments

Notes

  • Originally created as a hobby project to control RC vehicles from Android devices (January 2022)
  • Uses UDP for low-latency wireless communication
  • JSON protocol makes it easy to extend with additional sensors or controls
  • Can be adapted for any UDP-enabled remote device

Created: January 27, 2022
Last Updated: January 28, 2022

entry.title: JABEL 031 Power Supply Mod
entry.summary: JABEL 031 Modified Power Supply Overview This repository contains comprehensive documentation and design files for a modified JABEL 031 power supply unit. This is an electronics project that includes PCB layouts, schematics, component lists, and AutoCAD drawings for the external enclosure design. Created: June 25, 2021…
entry.content:
JABEL 031 Modified Power Supply

Overview

This repository contains comprehensive documentation and design files for a modified JABEL 031 power supply unit. This is an electronics project that includes PCB layouts, schematics, component lists, and AutoCAD drawings for the external enclosure design.

Created: June 25, 2021

 

Project Description

This project is dedicated to the design and modification of a JABEL 031 power supply unit. The modifications include:

  • Custom PCB Design - Multi-layer circuit board layout and component placement
  • Schematic Diagrams - Complete electrical circuit documentation
  • 3D Enclosure Design - AutoCAD drawings for the external housing
  • Component Documentation - Detailed parts lists and specifications

The project combines both electrical and mechanical design aspects to create a fully documented and reproducible power supply system.

Schematic

Layout

Repository Structure

Zasilacz_Jabel031_MOD/
├── README.md                          # This file
├── LISTA_ELEMENTÓW.docx               # Component list (Polish)
├── PDF/                               # PDF documentation
│   ├── LISTA_CZĘŚCI.pdf               # Parts list
│   ├── Schemat_ideowy_PDF.pdf         # Schematic diagram
│   ├── PCB.pdf                        # PCB layout diagram
│   ├── PCB_OPIS.pdf                   # PCB description/notes
│   ├── MULTI_PCB.pdf                  # Multi-layer PCB details
│   ├── Rozmieszczenie_elementów_1.pdf # Component placement part 1
│   ├── Rozmieszczenie_elementów_2.pdf # Component placement part 2
│   ├── Rozmieszczenie_elementów_3.pdf # Component placement part 3
│   └── Obudowa/                       # Enclosure design PDFs
├── pliki_autocad/                     # AutoCAD design files
│   ├── Panel_przedni_AUTOCAD.dwg      # Front panel design
│   └── Panel_przedni2.dwg             # Front panel design (variant)
└── .gitattributes                     # Git configuration
 

Documentation Files

PDF Documents

All technical documentation is stored in the PDF/ directory:

File Description
LISTA_CZĘŚCI.pdf Complete parts and components list with specifications
Schemat_ideowy_PDF.pdf Full electrical schematic diagram of the power supply
PCB.pdf Single-sided PCB layout diagram
PCB_OPIS.pdf PCB documentation, notes, and layer descriptions
MULTI_PCB.pdf Multi-layer PCB design details and specifications
Rozmieszczenie_elementów_1.pdf Component placement guide - Part 1
Rozmieszczenie_elementów_2.pdf Component placement guide - Part 2
Rozmieszczenie_elementów_3.pdf Component placement guide - Part 3
Obudowa/ Enclosure and housing design documentation

Excel / Word Documents

  • LISTA_ELEMENTÓW.docx - Component list in editable format (Microsoft Word)

Hardware Design

Electrical Design

The power supply consists of:

  1. Input Stage - Main power conditioning
  2. Transformation Stage - Voltage regulation and isolation
  3. Output Stage - Final power delivery and filtering
  4. Protection Stage - Over-current and thermal protection

Key Specifications:

  • Custom multi-layer PCB design
  • Multiple voltage output rails
  • Protection circuitry for safe operation
  • Optimized component placement for thermal management

Mechanical Design

The enclosure is designed using AutoCAD with:

  • Front Panel Design - User interface and connector placement
  • Housing Structure - Aluminum or plastic enclosure
  • Thermal Management - Ventilation and heat dissipation provisions

Design Files:

  • Panel_przedni_AUTOCAD.dwg - Original front panel design
  • Panel_przedni2.dwg - Alternative/updated front panel variant

Component List

A comprehensive list of all electronic components required for this project is documented in:

  • LISTA_ELEMENTÓW.docx - Editable component list with part numbers
  • PDF/LISTA_CZĘŚCI.pdf - PDF version of the components list

The list includes:

  • Resistors and capacitors (values and tolerances)
  • Active components (ICs, transistors, diodes)
  • Inductors and transformers
  • Connectors and mechanical parts
  • Specifications and reference designators

entry.title: HA_LAN_RGB_Dimmer_ESP8266
entry.summary: HA_LAN_RGB_Dimmer_ESP8266 An RGBW Smart Dimmer system using ESP8266 microcontroller, MQTT, and Home Assistant integration. HA_LAN_RGB_Dimmer_ESP8266 is an IoT device built on the ESP8266 microcontroller running MicroPython. It enables remote control of RGB/RGBW LED strips via MQTT and Home Assistant. This project allows you to control color, brightness,…
entry.content:
HA_LAN_RGB_Dimmer_ESP8266

An RGBW Smart Dimmer system using ESP8266 microcontroller, MQTT, and Home Assistant integration.

Overview

HA_LAN_RGB_Dimmer_ESP8266 is an IoT device built on the ESP8266 microcontroller running MicroPython. It enables remote control of RGB/RGBW LED strips via MQTT and Home Assistant. This project allows you to control color, brightness, and effects of LED lighting through MQTT messages or Home Assistant automation.

Tech Stack

  • Hardware: ESP8266 (WiFi microcontroller, e.g., NodeMCU, Wemos D1)
  • Language: Python (MicroPython on ESP8266)
  • Protocols: MQTT (remote communication), PWM (LED control), WiFi (network connectivity)
  • Integration: Home Assistant (MQTT discovery)
  • Libraries: umqttsimple (MQTT client), network utilities

Project Structure

.
├── boot.py              Initialization, configuration mode logic, WiFi connection
├── main.py              Main loop — listening for MQTT commands and updating LEDs
├── config.txt           Configuration file (WiFi, MQTT, GPIO pins)
└── index.html           Web interface for device configuration
 

How It Works

Startup & Configuration Mode

After startup, ESP8266 checks a GPIO pin. If the pin is shorted to GND, the device enters configuration mode:

  • Creates a WiFi network named ESP_RGB_Dimmer (no password)
  • Runs an HTTP server on port 80
  • Serves an HTML page (index.html) with a configuration form
  • Receives JSON configuration data and saves it to config.txt

Normal Operation Mode (Pin not shorted)

  • Loads configuration from config.txt
  • Connects to the configured WiFi network
  • Establishes connection with MQTT broker
  • main.py listens to MQTT topics for color and brightness commands
  • Updates PWM outputs to control RGB/RGBW LED strip colors and brightness
  • Supports dimming, color selection, and lighting effects

Getting Started

Step 1: Initial Configuration

  1. Upload all Python files to ESP8266
  2. Short configuration pin to GND
  3. Restart ESP8266

ESP8266 will boot in configuration mode:

  1. Connect to the ESP_RGB_Dimmer WiFi network
  2. Open your browser and navigate to http://192.168.1.1
  3. Fill out the configuration form:
    • GPIO Pins — R, G, B, W (white) channel pins
    • WiFi SSID — your network name
    • WiFi Password
    • MQTT Broker Address (e.g., mosquitto server, Home Assistant)
    • MQTT Topic — base topic for control (e.g., home/bedroom/rgb_light)
    • MQTT Username (if authentication required)
    • MQTT Password
  4. Click "Save"

Step 2: Run in Normal Mode

  1. Disconnect configuration pin from GND
  2. Restart ESP8266

The device will now boot in normal mode, connect to WiFi and MQTT. LED will blink to confirm connection.

Step 3: Control Your LEDs

Using mosquitto_pub:

# Set color to red (255, 0, 0) and brightness to 100%
mosquitto_pub -h <MQTT_SERVER> -t home/bedroom/rgb_light/set -m '{"r":255,"g":0,"b":0,"brightness":255}' -u <username> -P <password>

# Set color to blue
mosquitto_pub -h <MQTT_SERVER> -t home/bedroom/rgb_light/set -m '{"r":0,"g":0,"b":255,"brightness":255}' -u <username> -P <password>

# Dim to 50%
mosquitto_pub -h <MQTT_SERVER> -t home/bedroom/rgb_light/brightness -m '128' -u <username> -P <password>
 

Using Home Assistant:

service: mqtt.publish
data:
  topic: home/bedroom/rgb_light/set
  payload: '{"r":255,"g":0,"b":0,"brightness":255}'
 

Using Node-RED, Home Assistant, or any MQTT client:

  • Connect to your MQTT broker
  • Publish JSON messages or simple values to control:
    • Color values (RGB)
    • Brightness (0-255 PWM value)
    • On/Off commands

Features

  • 🌐 Remote LED control via MQTT from anywhere
  • 🎨 Full RGB/RGBW color control
  • 💡 Brightness dimming (0-255 PWM)
  • ⚙️ Easy web-based configuration interface
  • 🏠 Home Assistant integration via MQTT
  • 🔐 MQTT authentication support
  • 🔄 Automatic reconnection on connection loss
  • 📦 Minimal dependencies (MicroPython standard library)

Requirements

  • ESP8266 (NodeMCU, Wemos D1, or compatible)
  • MicroPython firmware
  • MQTT broker (mosquitto, Home Assistant, etc.)
  • RGB/RGBW LED strip (12V or 24V with PWM controller recommended)
  • Power supply for LED strip
  • Same local network for control

Configuration File Format

The config.txt file stores configuration in JSON format:

{
  "WiFissid": "your_wifi_name",
  "WiFipass": "your_wifi_password",
  "MQTTSrv": "mqtt.example.com",
  "MQTTBaseTopic": "home/bedroom/rgb_light",
  "MQTTUserName": "mqtt_user",
  "MQTTUserPass": "mqtt_password",
  "GPIO_R": 12,
  "GPIO_G": 13,
  "GPIO_B": 14,
  "GPIO_W": 15
}
 

MQTT Topics

Subscribe (device listens for commands):

  • {base_topic}/set — JSON with r, g, b, brightness
  • {base_topic}/color — HEX color (e.g., FF5500)
  • {base_topic}/brightness — Value 0-255
  • {base_topic}/on — Turn on (any value)
  • {base_topic}/off — Turn off

Publish (device reports state):

  • {base_topic}/state — Current color and brightness

Troubleshooting

  • Device won't connect to WiFi: Verify SSID and password are correct
  • MQTT connection fails: Check broker address, username, and password
  • LEDs not responding:
    • Verify GPIO pins are correct
    • Check MQTT topic matches configuration
    • Ensure PWM is working (test with digital multimeter)
    • Check LED strip power supply
  • Flickering or dim LEDs: Add capacitors (100µF) across power pins, use quality power supply

Hardware Wiring Example

ESP8266 Pin 12 (GPIO12) → MOSFET/PWM Controller (Red channel)
ESP8266 Pin 13 (GPIO13) → MOSFET/PWM Controller (Green channel)
ESP8266 Pin 14 (GPIO14) → MOSFET/PWM Controller (Blue channel)
ESP8266 Pin 15 (GPIO15) → MOSFET/PWM Controller (White channel)
ESP8266 GND → MOSFET GND, LED Strip GND
 

Future Enhancements

  • Support for lighting effects (fade, pulse, rainbow)
  • Multiple RGB strip groups
  • Brightness fade transitions
  • Color temperature control (Kelvin scale)
  • Hardware button controls
  • Local web dashboard for real-time control

License

This project is provided as-is for personal use.

Author

PawelZieba01

entry.title: WiFi_Thermometer-AP-WWW
entry.summary: WiFi_Termometer-AP-WWW A WiFi-enabled thermometer using ESP32 microcontroller with real-time temperature display and web interface. WiFi_Termometer-AP-WWW- is an IoT device built on the ESP32 (ESP-WROOM-32) microcontroller running MicroPython. It operates as a wireless access point and web server, reading temperature from a DS18B20 sensor and displaying it on…
entry.content:
WiFi_Termometer-AP-WWW

A WiFi-enabled thermometer using ESP32 microcontroller with real-time temperature display and web interface.

Overview

WiFi_Termometer-AP-WWW- is an IoT device built on the ESP32 (ESP-WROOM-32) microcontroller running MicroPython. It operates as a wireless access point and web server, reading temperature from a DS18B20 sensor and displaying it on a responsive web interface. Users connect to the device's WiFi network and access the live temperature display through any web browser.

Tech Stack

  • Hardware: ESP-WROOM-32 (WiFi microcontroller), DS18B20 (1-wire temperature sensor)
  • Language: Python (MicroPython on ESP32), HTML/JavaScript
  • Protocols: WiFi (Access Point mode), HTTP (web server), 1-wire (sensor communication), DNS
  • Libraries: onewire, ds18x20 (sensor protocol), machine (GPIO control), socket (HTTP server), network (WiFi AP)

Project Structure

.
├── main.py              Main application: WiFi AP setup, HTTP server, sensor reading loop
├── microDNSSrv.py       Custom DNS server for domain resolution (MIT licensed)
├── index.html           Web UI: real-time temperature display, JavaScript polling
└── README.md            Documentation
 

How It Works

Initialization & Setup

  1. ESP32 boots and initializes as a WiFi Access Point
  2. Creates WiFi network: "Termometr WI-FI" (no password required)
  3. Assigns IP: 192.168.2.1 (subnet: 255.255.255.0)
  4. Starts DNS server that resolves any domain to device IP
  5. Starts HTTP server on port 80 listening for connections
  6. Initializes DS18B20 temperature sensor on GPIO 15
  7. Sets up LED on GPIO 2 for status indication

Normal Operation

  • Browser Connection: User connects to "Termometr WI-FI" network and navigates to:

    • http://192.168.2.1 (direct IP)
    • http://termometr.pl or any domain (resolved by DNS server)

  • Web Interface: index.html loads and starts JavaScript polling

  • Temperature Reading: JavaScript sends /getTemp request every 1 second

  • Server Response: main.py receives request, reads DS18B20 sensor, returns:

    • Current time (HH:MM:SS format)
    • Temperature value (e.g., "23.5°C")

  • Display Update: Web page updates temperature and time in real-time

  • Error Handling: If sensor is disconnected, error message blinks on page

LED Status Signals

  • Blinks every 0.4s: Waiting for WiFi device to connect
  • Blinks every 1s: Taking temperature measurement and sending data
  • Blinks rapidly (0.1s intervals): Successful WiFi connection confirmed (~2 seconds)
  • Solid on: Temperature sensor not detected
  • Off: Normal operation, waiting for browser requests

Getting Started

Requirements

  • ESP32 (ESP-WROOM-32) with MicroPython firmware installed
  • DS18B20 temperature sensor
  • LED (optional, for status indication)
  • Wiring:
    • DS18B20 data pin → GPIO 15 (configurable in main.py)
    • DS18B20 GND → GND
    • DS18B20 VCC → 3.3V (with pull-up resistor ~4.7kΩ)
    • LED anode → GPIO 2, LED cathode → GND (optional)

Step 1: Upload Files to ESP32

  1. Upload the following files to ESP32 filesystem:

    • main.py
    • microDNSSrv.py
    • index.html

  2. Ensure MicroPython is installed on your ESP32

Step 2: Power On & Connect

  1. Power on the ESP32
  2. LED will blink every 0.4s while waiting for connection
  3. Open WiFi network list on your phone/computer
  4. Connect to "Termometr WI-FI" network (no password)
  5. Open web browser and navigate to:
    • http://192.168.2.1 (recommended)
    • http://termometr.pl (or any domain)

Step 3: View Temperature

  • Temperature and time update every 1 second
  • Large display: "Temperatura: XX.X°C"
  • Time display: "HH:MM:SS"
  • If sensor is missing: Error message blinks with red text

Features

  • 🌡️ Real-time temperature monitoring via DS18B20 sensor
  • 🌐 Web-based interface accessible from any device
  • 📱 Responsive design for mobile and desktop browsers
  • 🔗 WiFi Access Point mode (no router required)
  • 📡 DNS server (any domain resolves to device)
  • 💡 Visual LED status feedback
  • 📊 JavaScript polling (updates every 1 second)
  • 🛡️ Automatic error detection and display
  • ⚙️ Minimal dependencies (MicroPython standard library)

Configuration

Adjustable Parameters in main.py

# WiFi AP Settings
ap_ip = "192.168.2.1"         # Device IP address
ap_mask = "255.255.255.0"     # Subnet mask
ap_gate = "192.168.2.1"       # Gateway
ap_ssid = "Termometr WI-FI"   # WiFi network name

# Sensor Pin
ds_pin = machine.Pin(15)      # DS18B20 data pin (change if needed)

# DNS Configuration
termo_url = "termometr.*"     # Domain pattern (e.g., termometr.pl, termometr.com, etc.)

# LED Pin
led = Pin(2, Pin.OUT)         # Status LED on GPIO 2
 

Modify RTC Time

Current code sets fixed date (2014-05-01). To use current time:

# In main.py, line 130 - replace with:
import ntptime
ntptime.settime()  # Syncs with NTP server
 

Troubleshooting

  • Device won't show up in WiFi list:

    • Verify MicroPython is properly flashed
    • Check power supply (at least 500mA recommended)

  • Browser can't connect to device:

    • Verify you're on the "Termometr WI-FI" network
    • Try both 192.168.2.1 and termometr.pl
    • Restart ESP32

  • Temperature not updating:

    • Verify DS18B20 sensor wiring (especially pull-up resistor)
    • Check GPIO 15 is not used elsewhere
    • Look for error message on page

  • Sensor always shows error:

    • Verify DS18B20 is connected to GPIO 15
    • Try higher pull-up resistor (4.7kΩ - 10kΩ)
    • Check sensor is genuine (counterfeits common)
    • Reduce cable length if using long wires

  • LED doesn't work:

    • Verify LED polarity (longer leg to GPIO 2)
    • Try different GPIO pin and update main.py
    • LED is optional and doesn't affect thermometer

Future Enhancements

  • Support for multiple DS18B20 sensors
  • Temperature logging and history graph
  • Configurable polling interval
  • MQTT integration for remote monitoring
  • Email/SMS alerts for temperature thresholds
  • Dark mode for web interface
  • WiFi password protection

License

This project is provided as-is for personal use.

Author

PawelZieba01

Paweł Zięba - 05.04.2020

entry.title: Wake_On_Lan_ESP32
entry.summary: Wake_On_Lan_ESP32 A remote computer wake-up system using ESP32 microcontroller, MQTT, and Wake-on-LAN protocol. Wake_On_Lan_ESP32 is an IoT device built on the ESP32 (ESP-WROOM-32) microcontroller running MicroPython. It enables remote activation of a PC via MQTT and Wake-on-LAN protocol. This project solves the problem of remotely powering on…
entry.content:
Wake_On_Lan_ESP32

A remote computer wake-up system using ESP32 microcontroller, MQTT, and Wake-on-LAN protocol.

Overview

Wake_On_Lan_ESP32 is an IoT device built on the ESP32 (ESP-WROOM-32) microcontroller running MicroPython. It enables remote activation of a PC via MQTT and Wake-on-LAN protocol. This project solves the problem of remotely powering on a computer from outside its local network, bypassing the lack of a public IP address — the device acts as a communication bridge between an MQTT broker and the WoL protocol within the local network.

Tech Stack

  • Hardware: ESP-WROOM-32 (WiFi microcontroller)
  • Language: Python (MicroPython on ESP32)
  • Protocols: MQTT (remote communication), Wake-on-LAN (device activation), WiFi (network connectivity)
  • Libraries: umqttsimple (MQTT client), socket (network communication)

Project Structure

.
├── boot.py              Initialization, configuration mode logic, WiFi connection
├── main.py              Main loop — listening for MQTT commands and sending WoL packets
├── wakeonlan.py         Wake-on-LAN implementation (packet generation and transmission)
├── umqttsimple.py       MQTT client for broker communication
├── config.txt           Configuration file (WiFi, MQTT, target MAC address)
└── index.html           Web interface for device configuration
 

How It Works

Startup & Configuration Mode

After startup, ESP32 checks pin D4. If pin D4 is shorted to GND, the device enters configuration mode:

  • Creates a WiFi network named ESP_WakeOnLan (no password)
  • Runs an HTTP server on port 80
  • Serves an HTML page (index.html) with a configuration form
  • Receives JSON configuration data and saves it to config.txt

Normal Operation Mode (Pin D4 not shorted)

  • Loads configuration from config.txt
  • Connects to the configured WiFi network
  • Establishes connection with MQTT broker
  • main.py listens to the subscribed MQTT topic
  • When signal '1' is received, sends a Wake-on-LAN magic packet (wakeonlan.py) to the target device
  • LED blinks as confirmation of packet transmission

Getting Started

Step 1: Initial Configuration

  1. Upload all Python files to ESP32
  2. Short pin D4 to GND
  3. Restart ESP32

ESP32 will boot in configuration mode:

  1. Connect to the ESP_WakeOnLan WiFi network
  2. Open your browser and navigate to http://192.168.1.1
  3. Fill out the configuration form:
    • Target MAC Address of the computer to wake (format: AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF)
    • WiFi SSID — your network name
    • WiFi Password
    • MQTT Broker Address (e.g., mosquitto server, Home Assistant)
    • MQTT Topic — subscription topic (e.g., home/wol/pc)
    • MQTT Username (if authentication required)
    • MQTT Password
  4. Click "Save"

Step 2: Run in Normal Mode

  1. Disconnect pin D4 from GND
  2. Restart ESP32

The device will now boot in normal mode, connect to WiFi and MQTT. LED will blink twice to confirm connection.

Step 3: Wake Up Your Computer

Send message '1' to your MQTT topic:

Using mosquitto_pub:

mosquitto_pub -h <MQTT_SERVER> -t home/wol/pc -m '1' -u <username> -P <password>
 

Using Home Assistant:

service: mqtt.publish
data:
  topic: home/wol/pc
  payload: '1'
 

Using Node-RED, MQTT.fx, or any MQTT client:

  • Connect to your MQTT broker
  • Publish message '1' to your configured topic

ESP32 will send a magic packet to your local network, and your computer will power on (requires Wake-on-LAN enabled in BIOS).

Features

  • 🌐 Remote computer activation via MQTT from anywhere
  • ⚙️ Easy web-based configuration interface
  • 🔐 MQTT authentication support
  • 💡 Visual feedback via onboard LED
  • 🔄 Automatic reconnection on connection loss
  • 📦 Minimal dependencies (MicroPython standard library)

Requirements

  • ESP32 (ESP-WROOM-32) or compatible
  • MicroPython firmware
  • MQTT broker (mosquitto, Home Assistant, etc.)
  • Target computer with Wake-on-LAN enabled in BIOS
  • Same local network for target computer (ESP32 and computer must be in the same LAN)

Configuration File Format

The config.txt file stores configuration in JSON format:

{
  "WiFissid": "your_wifi_name",
  "WiFipass": "your_wifi_password",
  "MQTTSrv": "mqtt.example.com",
  "MQTTSubTopic": "home/wol/pc",
  "MQTTUserName": "mqtt_user",
  "MQTTUserPass": "mqtt_password",
  "RemoteMAC": "AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF"
}
 

Troubleshooting

  • Device won't connect to WiFi: Verify SSID and password are correct
  • MQTT connection fails: Check broker address, username, and password
  • Computer won't wake up:
    • Ensure Wake-on-LAN is enabled in BIOS
    • Verify target MAC address is correct
    • Check that ESP32 and computer are on the same network
  • LED doesn't blink: Verify LED is connected to GPIO2, or modify pin in boot.py

Future Enhancements

  • Support for multiple target MAC addresses
  • Device status monitoring via MQTT
  • Custom GPIO pin configuration
  • Wake-on-LAN settings (broadcast address, port) in web interface
  • Device health monitoring and error reporting

License

This project is provided as-is for personal use.

Author

PawelZieba01
